title: Controlled Vocabulary and Ontologies
text:
Controlled Vocabulary
Below you will explore some of the commonly used controlled vocabularies in biomedical data: ICD-10 for diseases, LOINC for procedures, …
For each of these vocabularies there are also different dedicated DB.
Here I use NLM Clinical Tables because it provides a simple unified interface: NLM Clinical Tables.

Diseases (ICD-10)

ICD history (WHO)

title: Controlled Vocabulary and Ontologies
text:
Ontologies
Large-scale effort to represent medical knowledge.

Snomed Browser
Snowstorm-Training

Here you will explore programmatic requests to Snowmed CT and ontology relationships.

FHIR queries

Search for ConceptID
Example: Identify the Snomed ID for the disorder concept “diabetes mellitus”


Code
concept = "diabetes mellitus"
params = {
    "url": "http://snomed.info/sct?fhir_vs",
    "filter": concept,
    "count": 20
}

r = requests.get(FHIR_URL, params=params)
r.raise_for_status()
results = r.json()["expansion"]["contains"]
pd.DataFrame(results).head()

Query ECL Template
Snomed CT codes form a large ontology. Queries into this data and be formulated with a so called Expression Constraint Language (ECL).


Code
def query_ecl(ecl_expression):
    """
    Sends ECL query to FHIR endpoint
    Returns list of concepts names     
    """
    params = {
        "url": f"http://snomed.info/sct?fhir_vs=ecl/{ecl_expression}",
        "count": 500
    }
    r = requests.get(FHIR_URL, params=params)
    r.raise_for_status()
    
    data = r.json() 
    # data.keys(): resourceType, url (=query), expansion

    # return list of concepts data["expansion"]["contains"]
    return data.get("expansion", {}).get("contains", [])


Simple Usage Example: retrieve a single concept


Code
disease = "73211009" # Diabetes mellitus

query = f"{disease}" # very basic ECL query
results = query_ecl(query)

pd.DataFrame(results)









system
code
display

http://snomed.info/sct

DM - Diabetes mellitus









Complex ECL queries
More complicated ECL queries can also be used to search and retrieve multiple (related) concepts and attributes from the Snomed Ontology, e.g.

concept: “{code}”
parents: “>{code}”
children: “<{code}”
more examples: ECL quick reference

all children diseases for disease “obesity”

import requests

title: Data Exploration
text:
Generate synthetic data
This data set will be used throughout this notebook.
The idea is to simulate the effect of a hypothetical drug treatment to lower HbA1c levels in patients with diabetes. We will then explore different ways to visualize the data and interpret the results.


Code
np.random.seed(42)
n = 30

# synthetic HbA1c-Werte [in %]
# normal 5%, ehoeht: 6%, Diabetes mellitus: > 6.5%, 8% Handlungsbedarf
before = np.random.normal(loc=9.0, scale=1, size=n)

# Two treatmentgroups
group = np.random.choice(["A", "B"], size=n, p=[0.25, 0.75])

after = before.copy()
after[group == "A"] -= np.random.normal(0.5, 0.1, size=(group == "A").sum())
after[group == "B"] += np.random.normal(0.0, 0.1, size=(group == "B").sum())

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "patient": np.arange(n),
    "before": before,
    "after": after,
    "group": group
})

df[["before","after"]].head()
#print(df[["before", "after"]].mean())
#df.groupby("group").describe().T

title: Data Exploration
text:
Data Reshaping
For some visualization tasks it will be better to have data in a long format. We will use the melt function from pandas to reshape the data. I use a categorical time variable to distinguish before and after treatment.


Code
long = df.melt(
        id_vars=["patient"],
        value_vars=["before", "after"],
        var_name="time",
        value_name="hba1c"
    )

# define a consitent order for the time variable
ORDER = ["before", "after"]
long["time"] = pd.Categorical(
    long["time"],
    categories=ORDER,
    ordered=True
)

long.head()

title: Data Exploration
text:
Barplots


Code
sns.barplot(data=long, x="time", y="hba1c", errorbar=None)
plt.ylim(8.6, 8.9)
plt.title("Clear effect on Mean")
plt.show()















CautionWhat is wrong with this plot ?








Code
sns.barplot(data=long, x="time", y="hba1c", errorbar=None)
plt.title("Almost no effect")
plt.show()









We tend to give meaning to barplot length, rather than position.
Until now we have focused on the mean values. But to discuss any potential treatment effect we will also have to analyse (and visualize) the variability.


Code
sns.barplot(data=long, x="time", y="hba1c", errorbar='se')
plt.title("Mean ± SE. Perhaps a small effect?")
plt.show()















NoteVariance, Standard Deviation & Standard Error





Variance Estimate: \[
\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^N (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \approx E[(X - \mu)^2]
\]
Standard Deviation (SD): \[
\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}
\]
Standard Error (SE): \[
SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}} \longrightarrow 0 \text{ for } N \rightarrow \infty
\]



The standard error (SE) of the mean is not a measure of variability in the data. For more data it will approach zero. The standard deviation (SD) is a better measure of variability in the data.


Code
sns.barplot(data=long, x="time", y="hba1c", errorbar='sd')
plt.title("Mean ± SD. Probably no effect.")
plt.show()











Code
# you are allowed to remove zero with boxplots
sns.boxplot(data=long, x="time", y="hba1c")
plt.title("Maybe some effect after all?")
plt.show()











Code
sns.violinplot(data=long, x="time", y="hba1c")
plt.title("Violin plot: Beware of smoothing artefacts!")
plt.show()









The violin plot employs some smoothing to visyualize the distribution. For small data sets this can be misleading. If possible, it is always better to show the data.
A statistical test can be used to support the visual impression.


Code
sns.stripplot( data=long, x="time", y="hba1c",
    # optional: switch off jitter
    jitter=False
)

t, p = ttest_ind( df["before"], df["after"])
title = f"Show data & test. p-value = {p:.5f}"
plt.title(title)
plt.show()









In the above we have assumed independent samples. But since we have repeated measures (before and after treatment) we should use a paired test. Here I also visualize the dependencies by connecting related data points explicitly with lines. This is only possible if we have a unique identifier for each patient (here: patient variable).


Code
sns.lineplot(data=long, x="time", y="hba1c",
    units="patient",
    estimator=None,
    marker="o"
)
t, p = ttest_rel( df["before"], df["after"])
title = f"Data Dependencies? p-value {p:.5f}"
plt.title(title)
plt.show()









There seems to be a significant reduction in HbA1c levels for some patients. Are there other variables that can explain this difference?


Code
long_group = df.melt(
        id_vars=["patient","group"],
        value_vars=["before", "after"],
        var_name="time",
        value_name="hba1c"
    )

ORDER = ["before", "after"]
long_group["time"] = pd.Categorical(
    long_group["time"],
    categories=ORDER,
    ordered=True
)

sns.lineplot(data=long_group, x="time", y="hba1c",
    units="patient",
    estimator=None,
    marker="o",
    hue="group"
)
t, p = ttest_rel( df["before"], df["after"])
title = f"Discover new variables"
plt.title(title)
plt.show()









But is this also a clinically relevant effect? We should always be careful to distinguish statistical significance from medical significance.






ImportantSummary




Don’t cut barplots !
Show variability in data (clarify SE or SD)
better show the range \(\to\) boxplots & violinplots
beware of smoothing violins (especially for small data sets)
show all the data if you can \(\to\) stripplot
multimodal distribution & additional group variables?
data exploration is visual and iterative
statistical significance \(\ne\) medical significance

title: Data Visualization Tasks
text:
Task 2: Pie2Bar
Marginal and conditional distributions for categorical variables are often visualized as Pie charts.
The following would visualize the proportions of obesity categories given a gender. \[
Pr(\mbox{Obesity Category}| \mbox{Gender})
\]


Code
var1, var2 = "Gender", "NObeyesdad"
tab = pd.crosstab(df[var1], df[var2], normalize="index")

# notice transposition tab.T
axes = tab.T.plot.pie(
    subplots=True,
    figsize=FIGSIZE,
    legend=False,
)
plt.show()





Conditional distributions of obesity by gender




However this is not a very good visual encoding.

Explain why pie charts are difficult to interpret and compare visually.
Replace the pie chart with a stacked barplot to compare the distribution of obesity category by Gender

title: Data Visualization Tasks
text:
Task 3: Risk Factors & UpSets
Several variables in the obesity dataset describe patient-level risk factors or behaviours. They can be encoded as Boolean variables and they define sets of individuals based on the Boolean status (True / False).


Code
from venn import venn

risk_vars = ["family_history_with_overweight", "FAVC", "SMOKE", "SCC"]
risk_bool = df[risk_vars].eq("yes") # convert to Boolean
risk_bool.head(3)

# construct sets of elements (= individuals)
elements = risk_bool.index # all indicies of risk_bool
venn_sets = {
    col: set(elements[risk_bool[col]])
    for col in risk_bool.columns
}

plt.figure(figsize=FIGSIZE)
venn(venn_sets)
plt.title("Venn Diagram")
plt.show()


<Figure size 1050x600 with 0 Axes>









Based on this Venn Diagram

Determine the total elements (individuals) in each set
How many individuals smoke and monitor their caloric intake (SCC=True)
Explain why a Venn diagram becomes hard to read when several sets are compared
Prepare an UpSet plot for the same analysis and sort by intersect size (cardinality)

You can use this package and data preparation


Code
# if needed
# !pip install upsetplot

from upsetplot import UpSet, from_indicators

warnings.filterwarnings(
    "ignore",
    category=FutureWarning,
    module="upsetplot"
)

# define multi-index of Booleans

# count multi-index
upset_counts = upset_data.index.value_counts()

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns

FIGSIZE = (7, 4)

title: Data Visualization
text:
Scenario
A health minister was told that hospital-associated infections are a major concern in health care. She orders a review of all hospitals who submit the conditions of all their current patients in standardized ICD-10 format.
The minister asks:

“Which hospital looks most worrying in this report?”


Group Challenge
Imagine you are the analyst team preparing a short briefing for the minister.
You have obtained a long table of patient-level data from all hospitals in the following format (first few rows shown)


Code
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)

# hospital sizes
N = {"A": 10000, "B": 5000, "C": 500}

# infection rates
p = {
    "A": 0.10,  
    "B": 0.12,
    "C": 0.12
}

dfs = []

for hospital, n in N.items():

    condition = rng.choice(
        ["Y95", "Other"],
        size=n,
        p=[p[hospital], 1 - p[hospital]]
    )

    dfs.append(
        pd.DataFrame({
            "hospital": hospital,
            "condition": condition
        })
    )

df = pd.concat(dfs, ignore_index=True)
df.head()

For simplicity:

ImportantGroup Task (5min)

What type of variables are available in the data

title: Data Visualization
text:
Base line - prevalence
First we would probably want to have a baseline of


Code
condition_table = df["condition"].value_counts(dropna=True)
print(condition_table)


condition
Other    13887
Y95       1613
Name: count, dtype: int64


Notice: for simplicity, in this example there are only two conditions (“Y95” and “Other”). In general there would be many more conditions.
It might be easier to report this as proportions

condition_table = df["condition"].value_counts(dropna=True, normalize=True)
print(condition_table)


condition
Other    0.895935
Y95      0.104065
Name: proportion, dtype: float64


Notice: This is called the marginal distribution, because we ignore the variable hospital.
Jargon: summed over (=marginalized) over all hospitals

title: Data Visualization
text:
Conditional Distribution
The minister (and we) suspect a different number of patients with condition=“Y95” in different hopsitals. Let’s first filter the data for the condition of interest.

df_sub.head()

Now let’s summarize this subset of patient-wise data per hospital (and normalize)

# bars and stacked bars would be better still
# count_summary.plot(kind="bar")
# count_summary.to_frame().T.plot(kind="bar", stacked=True)

ImportantQuestions

Which hospital is the worst?

title: Data Visualization
text:
Marginal Distribution
Just like with the prior probability for condition we should also take into account the number of patients per hospital. This is equivalent to summing (marginalizing) over all conditions
\[
Pr(\mbox{Hospital}) = \sum_{\mbox{condition}} Pr(\mbox{Hospital, condition})
\]


Code
marg_summary = df["hospital"].value_counts()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=FIGSIZE)
count_summary.plot.pie(autopct="%1.1f%%", ax=ax[0])
marg_summary.plot.pie(autopct="%1.1f%%", ax=ax[1])
ax[0].set_title('Conditional Distribution \n (cond="Y95")')
ax[1].set_title('Marginal Distribution \n (cond="All)')
plt.show()





Marginal Distributions change our expectation










NoteConditional Challenges




Marginal distributions define our expectations: more patients \(\to\) more diseases
P(Hosptial|Condition) \(\ne\) Pr(Condition|Hospital)





Code
# this will only calculate row margins, since each column (hospital) is normalized to 1
tab = pd.crosstab(df["condition"], df["hospital"], 
    normalize="columns", margins=True)
fig, axes = plt.subplots( 2, 2, figsize=(8, 8))
axes = axes.flatten()

for ax, col in zip(axes, tab.columns):
    tab[col].plot.pie(
        ax=ax,
        legend=False,
        explode=[0.1, 0.0]
    )
    ax.set_title(col)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





P(condition|Hospital)










NotePie Challenges




not good for many categories
not good for comparisons (areas)

title: Data Visualization
text:
Joint Distribution
Conditional Distributions are confusing. Let’s try to visualize all proportions at once


Code
ct = pd.crosstab(df["hospital"], df["condition"], normalize="all")
plt.figure(figsize=FIGSIZE)
sns.heatmap(ct, annot=True, fmt=".3f", cmap="RdYlGn")
plt.show()















ImportantQuestion



What is the problem with this visualization?



Colour-encoding of numbers has to be done carefully.
Consider colour blindness, meaning of divergent colours?
area has no meaning



Code
plt.figure(figsize=FIGSIZE)
sns.heatmap(ct, annot=True, fmt=".3f", cmap="Blues")
plt.show()





Better choice of colours




Another useful encoding can be by area size (Mosaic plot)


Code
from statsmodels.graphics.mosaicplot import mosaic

#plt.figure(figsize=FIGSIZE)
mosaic(df, index=["hospital", "condition"], gap=0.01, title="Mosaic Plot")
plt.show()





Area-encoding can also be useful










TipJoint and Margins





Sometimes it is useful to show the joint distributions with the margins


Code
jdm = pd.crosstab(df["hospital"], df["condition"], normalize="all", margins=True)
print('Joint Distribution with Margins: \n', jdm)


Joint Distribution with Margins: 
 condition     Other       Y95       All
hospital                               
A          0.581677  0.063484  0.645161
B          0.285935  0.036645  0.322581
C          0.028323  0.003935  0.032258
All        0.895935  0.104065  1.000000


Based on the margins we can also calculate the expected proportion of in a given cell.
For example:
\[
\begin{array}{ll}
Pr(\mbox{Hospital=A and Condition=Y95}) = \\

title: Visualizing Numerical Data
text:
Generate synthetic data
This data set will be used throughout this notebook.
The idea is to simulate the effect of a hypothetical drug treatment to lower HbA1c levels in patients with diabetes. We will then explore different ways to visualize the data and interpret the results.


Code
np.random.seed(42)
n = 30

# synthetic HbA1c-Werte [in %]
# normal 5%, ehoeht: 6%, Diabetes mellitus: > 6.5%, 8% Handlungsbedarf
before = np.random.normal(loc=9.0, scale=1, size=n)

# Two treatmentgroups
group = np.random.choice(["A", "B"], size=n, p=[0.25, 0.75])

after = before.copy()
after[group == "A"] -= np.random.normal(0.5, 0.1, size=(group == "A").sum())
after[group == "B"] += np.random.normal(0.0, 0.1, size=(group == "B").sum())

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "patient": np.arange(n),
    "before": before,
    "after": after,
    "group": group
})

df[["before","after"]].head()
#print(df[["before", "after"]].mean())
#df.groupby("group").describe().T

Data Reshaping
For some visualization tasks it will be better to have data in a long format. We will use the melt function from pandas to reshape the data. I use a categorical time variable to distinguish before and after treatment.

        id_vars=["patient"],
        value_vars=["before", "after"],
        var_name="time",

    )

# define a consitent order for the time variable
ORDER = ["before", "after"]

    long["time"],

title: Visualizing Numerical Data
text:
Barplot Stories


Code
sns.barplot(data=long, x="time", y="hba1c", errorbar=None)
plt.ylim(8.6, 8.9)
plt.title("Clear effect on Mean")
plt.show()















CautionWhat is wrong with this plot ?








Code
sns.barplot(data=long, x="time", y="hba1c", errorbar=None)
plt.title("Almost no effect")
plt.show()









We tend to give meaning to barplot length, rather than position.
Until now we have focused on the mean values. But to discuss any potential treatment effect we will also have to analyse (and visualize) the variability.


Code
sns.barplot(data=long, x="time", y="hba1c", errorbar='se')
plt.title("Mean ± SE. Perhaps a small effect?")
plt.show()















NoteVariance, Standard Deviation & Standard Error





Variance Estimate: \[
\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^N (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \approx E[(X - \mu)^2]
\]
Standard Deviation (SD): \[
\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}
\]
Standard Error (SE): \[
SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}} \longrightarrow 0 \text{ for } N \rightarrow \infty
\]



The standard error (SE) of the mean is not a measure of variability in the data. For more data it will approach zero. The standard deviation (SD) is a better measure of variability in the data.


Code
sns.barplot(data=long, x="time", y="hba1c", errorbar='sd')
plt.title("Mean ± SD. Probably no effect.")
plt.show()











Code
# you are allowed to remove zero with boxplots
sns.boxplot(data=long, x="time", y="hba1c")
plt.title("Maybe some effect after all?")
plt.show()











Code
sns.violinplot(data=long, x="time", y="hba1c")
plt.title("Violin plot: Beware of smoothing artefacts!")
plt.show()









The violin plot employs some smoothing to visyualize the distribution. For small data sets this can be misleading. If possible, it is always better to show the data.
A statistical test can be used to support the visual impression.


Code
sns.stripplot( data=long, x="time", y="hba1c",
    # optional: switch off jitter
    jitter=False
)

t, p = ttest_ind( df["before"], df["after"])
title = f"Show data & test. p-value = {p:.5f}"
plt.title(title)
plt.show()









In the above we have assumed independent samples. But since we have repeated measures (before and after treatment) we should use a paired test. Here I also visualize the dependencies by connecting related data points explicitly with lines. This is only possible if we have a unique identifier for each patient (here: patient variable).


Code
sns.lineplot(data=long, x="time", y="hba1c",
    units="patient",
    estimator=None,
    marker="o"
)
t, p = ttest_rel( df["before"], df["after"])
title = f"Data Dependencies? p-value {p:.5f}"
plt.title(title)
plt.show()









There seems to be a significant reduction in HbA1c levels for some patients. Are there other variables that can explain this difference?


Code
long_group = df.melt(
        id_vars=["patient","group"],
        value_vars=["before", "after"],
        var_name="time",
        value_name="hba1c"
    )

ORDER = ["before", "after"]
long_group["time"] = pd.Categorical(
    long_group["time"],
    categories=ORDER,
    ordered=True
)

sns.lineplot(data=long_group, x="time", y="hba1c",
    units="patient",
    estimator=None,
    marker="o",
    hue="group"
)
t, p = ttest_rel( df["before"], df["after"])
title = f"Discover new variables"
plt.title(title)
plt.legend(bbox_to_anchor=(1.05, 1), title="group")
plt.show()









But is this also a clinically relevant effect? We should always be careful to distinguish statistical significance from medical significance.






ImportantSummary




Don’t cut barplots !
Show variability in data (clarify SE or SD)
better show the range \(\to\) boxplots & violinplots
beware of smoothing violins (especially for small data sets)
show all the data if you can \(\to\) stripplot
multimodal distribution & additional group variables?
data exploration is visual and iterative
statistical significance \(\ne\) medical significance

text:
Effective Data Encoding



Automating the Design of Graphic Presentations. Jock Mackinlay (1987). Computer Science ACM Trans. Graph








Important




Position is most effective data encoding
Colour hue can be good to encode categorical differences





Code
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid", context="notebook")
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)

FIGSIZE = (7, 4)

title: Principles of Data Visualization
text:
Proportional Data
Pie charts are frequently used to illustrate proportions that should sum to 1. This can work for only the simplest cases, but in general it will be difficult to compare different pie chart


Code
df_count = pd.DataFrame({
    "group1": [20,5,18,5,18,12,5],
    "group2": [20,5,15,5,18,12,8],
}, index=list("ABCDEFG"))

fig, ax = plt.subplots( 1, 3, figsize=FIGSIZE, 
    gridspec_kw={"width_ratios": [1, 1, 2]})

df_count.plot.pie(y="group1", ax=ax[0], legend=False)
df_count.plot.pie(y="group2", ax=ax[1], legend=False)
df_count.T.plot(kind="bar", stacked=True, legend=False, ax=ax[2])

fig.suptitle("Which counts have changed?")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









Principle: Areas are not the best encoding for quantitative values and arc elements are difficult to compare.
Stacked bar charts with a consistent ordering of categories are easier to read.
Replace pie charts! Use stacked bar charts when comparing sizes across groups.



Honesty Principle. Don’t even think about 3D pie charts!

title: Principles of Data Visualization
text:
Bars and Boxes
A bar plot of means hides the actual observations. Two groups can have similar means and standard deviations but very different distributions.


Code
n = 60
df_bin = pd.DataFrame({
    "group": np.repeat(["A", "B"], n),
    "value": np.r_[
        rng.normal(10, 1.2, n),
        np.r_[rng.normal(8.5, 0.4, n // 2), rng.normal(11.5, 0.4, n // 2)]
    ]
})

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=FIGSIZE, sharey=True)
sns.barplot(data=df_bin, x="group", y="value", ax=axes[0])
axes[0].set_title("Poor: mean ± SD only")

sns.stripplot(data=df_bin, x="group", y="value", alpha=0.7, jitter=0.25, ax=axes[1])
sns.boxplot(data=df_bin, x="group", y="value", color="lightgray", ax=axes[1])
# boxplots show IQR between Q1 (25%) and Q3 (75%)
axes[1].set_title("Better: show observations")
plt.tight_layout()















Important




also boxplots maybe misleading for multimodal data.
Beware of meaningless means and erroneous errorbars!



Principle: show raw observations when sample size permits.

title: Principles of Data Visualization
text:
Colours

are not the most suitable way to encode quantitative values.
may improve visual encoding of categorical variables.







Caution



Do I need colour encoding at all?



Do not reinvent the colour wheel
Colour palettes can be customized, but with great colour power comes great responsibility. Suitable colour palettes have been studied and optimized for a long time. Do not waste your time!

    ax=axes[0],
)
axes[0].set_title("Avoid: arbitrary colours")

# Better: tested qualitative palette
sns.boxplot(
    data=df, x="group", y="after",
    hue="group",
    palette=good_palette,
    ax=axes[1],
)

axes[1].set_title("Better: ColorBrewer Set2")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









Question: Do you really need a colour encoding here?
Principle: use pre-defined colour palettes e.g. ColorBrewer


Don’t use bidirectional colour scales for unidirectional data
Heatmaps are popular tools to encode numerical data. A diverging color scale implies a meaningful center, often zero.


Code
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
corr = pd.DataFrame(rng.uniform(low=-1, high=1, size=(6, 6)))
heat = pd.DataFrame(rng.gamma(shape=2, scale=2, size=(6, 6)))

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=FIGSIZE)


sns.heatmap(heat, cmap="coolwarm", vmin=0, 
    square=True, cbar_kws={"shrink":0.6}, ax=axes[0])
axes[0].set_title("Misleading. \n Data is strictlypositive")

sns.heatmap(heat, cmap="Blues", vmin=0, 
    square=True, cbar_kws={"shrink":0.6}, ax=axes[1])
axes[1].set_title("Better. \n For sequential scale")

#sns.heatmap(corr, cmap="coolwarm", vmin=-1, vmax=1, 
#    square=True, cbar_kws={"shrink":0.6},
#    ax=axes[2])
#axes[2].set_title("OK. \n Data has true center at zero")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









Principle: Use divergent colours only for data with a meaningful center.

title: Principles of Data Visualization
text:
Summarizing Small Samples
Small sample sizes can be a problem for histograms (bin sensitivty), violin plots (smoothing artefacts), and bar plots (misleading means and error bars).


Code
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)

n = 10
small_n = pd.DataFrame({
    "group": np.repeat(["A", "B"], n),
    "value": np.r_[rng.normal(0, 1, n), rng.normal(0, 1, n)]
})

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=FIGSIZE, sharey=True)

sns.histplot(data=small_n, y="value", hue="group", alpha=0.5, ax=axes[0])
axes[0].set_title("Sensitive to binning")

sns.violinplot(data=small_n, x="group", y="value", ax=axes[1])
axes[1].set_title("Sensitive to smoothing")

sns.stripplot(data=small_n, x="group", y="value", ax=axes[2])
axes[2].set_title("Better: show every point")
plt.tight_layout()









Principle: Show data points whenever possible.

df_int = df.astype(int)
inter = df_int.T.dot(df_int) # df_int.T @ df_int

    annot=True, fmt="d", cmap="Blues", vmin=0,
    square=True, cbar_kws={"shrink":0.7},

    ax=ax[1,0]
)

# union(A,B) = size(A) + size(B) - intersect(A,B)
sizes = df_int.sum().values
union = sizes[:, None] + sizes[None, :] - inter
jaccard = inter / union

# same from sklearn

sns.heatmap(jaccard,

title: Transforming Numerical Variables
text:
Variable Transformations

Scaling (numerical variables)
The most common normalizations techniques are Standardization/Normalization
\[
X \to Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}
\]


Code
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

# numeric: standardization
scaler = StandardScaler()
Z = scaler.fit_transform(df[num_cols]) # Z: numpy array !


and Min-Max scaling: \[
X \to Z = \frac{X - X_{min}}{X_{max} - X_{min}}
\]


Code
# alternative scaling only shown for illustration 
# not executed
from sklearn.preprocessing import MinMaxScaler

scaler = MinMaxScaler(feature_range=(0, 1))
Z = scaler.fit_transform(df[num_cols])




Inverse Scaling
Scale transformations are invertible
\[
X = Z \sigma + \mu
\]
or
\[
X = Z (X_{max} - X_{min}) + X_{min}
\]


Code
Z_inv = scaler.inverse_transform(Z)

if np.testing.assert_allclose(df[num_cols], Z_inv) is None:
    print("Scaling is invertible")


Scaling is invertible




Code
num_norm = ( 
  pd.DataFrame(Z, columns=num_cols)
  .melt(var_name="variable", value_name="value")
)

plt.figure(figsize=FIGSIZE)
sns.violinplot(data=num_norm, x="variable", y="value")
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.ylabel("Z-score")
plt.title("Scaled Violins: Standardized Variables")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()















NoteNotice




Z- or MinMax scaling does not change shape of distribtuion
it only shifts the mean and changes the scale
Z is dimensionless
Standardization centers data around mean=0 and scales to unit variance (std=1)
Useful for algorithms that depend on distances between samples (e.g. clustering)









ImportantQuestion



What could be the reason that not all distributions are unimodal?








NoteDealing with “Unnormality”





Often variables do not show Gaussian (Normal) Distributions:

outliers? measurement errors?
non-Gaussian processes: (multiplicative) growth (e.g population size, income distribution, …)
integers: discrete values (e.g. count data, age in years)
multimodality: other variables (i.e not just random noise)?






Code
df_norm = pd.DataFrame(Z, columns=num_cols)
df_norm["Obesity"] = df["Obesity"]

plt.figure(figsize=FIGSIZE)
sns.violinplot(data=df_norm, x="Obesity", y="Weight", hue="Obesity")
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.ylabel("Normalized Weight (Z-score)")
plt.title("Multimodality and Other Factors")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





Multimodality in Weight is clearly due to other factors






Other Transformations

Sqrt-Transformation
Count data often follows a Poisson distribution with some parameter \(\lambda\)
\[
P(X=k) = \frac{\lambda^k e^{-\lambda}} {k!}
\]
For this distribution the variance increases with the mean \[
Var(X) = E[X] = \lambda
\] This is undesirable as large expected counts tend to have larger variance
For a general variable transformation \(x \to z=f(x)\) we have
\[
Var(Z) = |f'(x)|^2 Var(X)
\]
It turns out that square-root transformations can stabilize the variance
\[
z = 2 \sqrt{x} \to Var(Z) = 1
\]






TipVariance Stabilization Illustration







Code
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)

# generate Poisson data with 3 means (lambdas)
N = 1000 # number of observations
lambdas = [10, 50, 100]

# define groups g with mean mu
df = pd.DataFrame({"g": np.repeat(lambdas, N)})

# x-values drawn from Poisson
df["x"] = np.concatenate([
    rng.poisson(lam=l, size=N) for l in lambdas
])

# y-values from transformation
df["y"] = 2 * np.sqrt(df["x"])
df["g"] = df["g"].astype(str)

def marginal_scatter(data, ylabel):
    g = sns.JointGrid(
        data=data,
        x="x",
        y="y",
        height=6,
        ratio=5,
        space=0.05
    )

    sns.scatterplot(
        data=data,
        x="x",
        y="y",
        hue="g",
        ax=g.ax_joint
    )

    sns.histplot(
        data=data,
        x="x",
        hue="g",
        bins=100,
        ax=g.ax_marg_x,
        legend=False
    )

    sns.histplot(
        data=data,
        y="y",
        hue="g",
        bins=100,
        ax=g.ax_marg_y,
        legend=False
    )

    g.figure.suptitle("Variance Stabilization", fontsize=16)
    g.ax_joint.set_xlim(0, 125)
    g.ax_joint.set_xlabel("X")
    g.ax_joint.set_ylabel(ylabel)
    g.ax_joint.legend(title=r"$\lambda$", loc="best")

    return g

g1 = marginal_scatter(df, r"$Z = 2*\sqrt{X}$")
plt.show()












Real World Example: Number of Medications
This is from a large dataset for diabetes patients


Code
# large dataset of patients diagnosed with diabetes (130 US hopsitals)
# paper: https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1155/2014/781670
dataset = fetch_ucirepo(id=296) 

# of interest here are the number of medications
VAR="num_medications"
VAR_TRANS="num_medications_sqrt"
X = pd.DataFrame({
    VAR: dataset.data.features[VAR],
    VAR_TRANS: 2*np.sqrt(dataset.data.features[VAR]) # sqrt-transform
})




Code
fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=FIGSIZE)

sns.violinplot(data=X, y=VAR, ax=ax[0])
ax[0].set_title("Original: skewed distribution")

sns.violinplot(data=X, y=VAR_TRANS, ax=ax[1])
ax[1].set_title("Square-root transformed")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()











Log-Transformation
Other common transformations include log transformation which can also be applied to skewed data.
This can be expected when data derives from multiplicative growth processes (population, wealth, cancer etc.). This frequently gives rise to log-normal distribution with large skew.
Example: Mean Area (for breast cancer samples)


Code
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer

df = load_breast_cancer(as_frame=True).frame

VAR="mean area"
VAR_TRANS = "mean area (log1p)"

X = pd.DataFrame({
    VAR: df[VAR],
    VAR_TRANS: np.log1p(df[VAR])
})


fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=FIGSIZE)
sns.violinplot(data=X, y=VAR, ax=ax[0])
ax[0].set_title("Original: skewed distribution")

sns.violinplot(data=X, y=VAR_TRANS, ax=ax[1])
ax[1].set_title("Log transformed")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()











Appendix: Box-Cox Transformation
a generalization of Log-transformation is the Box-Cox transformation (for strictly positive data)
\[
y = x(\lambda) = \begin{cases}
\frac{x^\lambda - 1}{\lambda} & \text{if } \lambda \neq 0 \\
\log(x) & \text{if } \lambda = 0
\end{cases}
\]
Frequently Box-Cox transformed are also standardized \(x \to y \to z\)

Filter: Select numeric data with positive values


Code
# pick some numerical columns (mean values)
df = df.filter(regex=r"^mean") 

# ensure all columns are positive (or drop)
pos_cols = df.columns[df.gt(0).all() & df.notna().all()] 
X = df[pos_cols]




Box-Cox (sklearn): Determine optimal \(\lambda\)


Code
from sklearn.preprocessing import PowerTransformer

Data (scaled)
The Breast Cancer Wisconsin dataset contains 30 features (obtained from images) describing characteristics of the cell nuclei, along with a binary diagnosis (malignant/benign).

Pairwise Distances and Similarities

Between variables
\[d_{k, l} = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - Cor(Z_k, Z_l)\right) \in [0, 1]\]

\]

title: Data Clustering
text:
Clustering Approaches

Hierarchical Clustering
Hierarchical clustering requires:

a distance definition, for example Euclidean distance
an agglomeration method

complete linkage: cluster distance is maximal distance between cluster members
average linkage: cluster distance is average distance between cluster members

optional: cut prescription, that is, at which height to cut the tree







TipAgglomeration Methods





Assume that distance between all elements are defined \(d(a,b)\) and initially each elements defines its own cluster.
Clusters are merged iteratively - nearest clusters first: \(C = A\cup B\).
New distances \(D(C,X)\) between merged clusters \(C\) and old cluster \(X\) can be defined in various ways. For example:







Method
Update rule (\(C=A\cup B\))




Single linkage
\(D(C,X)=\min\!\left(D(A,X),\,D(B,X)\right)\)


Complete linkage
\(D(C,X)=\max\!\left(D(A,X),\,D(B,X)\right)\)


WPGMA (weighted average linkage)
\(D(C,X)=\displaystyle\frac{D(A,X)+D(B,X)}{2}\)


UPGMA (average linkage)
\(D(C,X)=\displaystyle\frac{|A|\,D(A,X)+|B|\,D(B,X)}{|A|+|B|}\)








Code
L = linkage(Z, method="complete", metric="euclidean")

# cut tree and define number of clusters
THRESH=10
cluster_hclust = fcluster(L, t=THRESH, criterion="distance")
n_clusters = len(np.unique(cluster_hclust))
ann["Clusters"] = pd.Categorical(cluster_hclust)

plt.figure(figsize=FIGSIZE)
dendrogram(L, no_labels=True, color_threshold=THRESH)
plt.title(f"Hierarchical clustering. {n_clusters} clusters @ threshold = {THRESH}")
plt.xlabel("Samples")
plt.ylabel("Distance")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





Hierarchical Clustering. Distance between samples (“leaves”) is the total length along the dendrogram (“tree”). Notice that the ordering of leaves is arbitrary.




The height at which two branches merge in the tree corresponds to the distance between the two clusters being merged, as defined by the chosen linkage criterion.
Notice that the order of branches and nodes in these hierarchical trees (“dendrograms”)is arbitrary: branches can be flipped or rotated.


Code
cluster_palette = dict(zip(
    ann["Clusters"].unique(),
    sns.color_palette("tab10", ann["Clusters"].nunique())

NoteInterpretation




Unsupervised clustering (with user-defined threshold!) results in 3 clusters (A, B, C)
A (blue). Malignant cells with large sizes (mean radius etc) and high mean smoothness, compactness, symmetry
B (green). Benign cells with small sizes and low mean smoothness
C (orange). Both benign and malignant cells with relatively large sizes and low smoothness etc.
other clustering threshold may result in different assignments or subgroups.





k-means Clustering

A visualization
The next example implements the core k-means iterations manually so that we can animate the assignment and centroid-update steps. This is pedagogically useful because scikit-learn’s KMeans returns the final result, not every intermediate step.


Code
def run_kmeans_history(data, k=2, max_iter=10, random_state=123):
    """
    Run a simple k-means loop in D dimensions.

    Parameters
    ----------
    data : numpy array, shape (n_samples, n_features)
        Numerical feature matrix.
    k : int
        Number of clusters.
    max_iter : int
        Maximum number of k-means iterations.
    random_state : int
        Random seed.

    Returns
    -------
    points : DataFrame
        Original data repeated for every iteration, with cluster labels.
    centers : DataFrame
        Centroid coordinates for every iteration.
    """
    rng = np.random.default_rng(random_state)
    X = np.asarray(data)

    n_samples, n_features = X.shape

    feature_names = [f"dim_{i}" for i in range(n_features)]

    centers = rng.uniform(
        low=X.min(axis=0),
        high=X.max(axis=0),
        size=(k, n_features),
    )

    point_frames = []
    center_frames = []

    for iteration in range(1, max_iter + 1):
        # E-step: assign samples to nearest centroid
        distances = ((X[:, None, :] - centers[None, :, :]) ** 2).sum(axis=2)
        labels = distances.argmin(axis=1)

        points = pd.DataFrame(X, columns=feature_names)
        points["sample_id"] = np.arange(n_samples)
        points["cluster"] = labels.astype(str)
        points["iteration"] = iteration
        point_frames.append(points)

        centroids = pd.DataFrame(centers, columns=feature_names)
        centroids["sample_id"] = "centroid_" + pd.Series(range(k)).astype(str)
        centroids["cluster"] = [str(i) for i in range(k)]
        centroids["iteration"] = iteration
        center_frames.append(centroids)

        # M-step: update centroid positions
        new_centers = centers.copy()

        for j in range(k):
            if np.any(labels == j):
                new_centers[j] = X[labels == j].mean(axis=0)

        if np.allclose(new_centers, centers):
            break

        centers = new_centers

    return (
        pd.concat(point_frames, ignore_index=True),
        pd.concat(center_frames, ignore_index=True),
    )




Code
# Select 2 meaningful features for visualization: mean radius and mean texture
X_2d = Z[["mean radius", "mean texture"]].to_numpy()

points, centers = run_kmeans_history(

    max_iter=10,
    random_state=42,
)

\(D=2\) is chosen only for illustration. In general distances and centroids are defined in any dimensions \(D\)








NoteLoss and Some Maths





The Loss Function
\[L = \sum_{l=1}^k \sum_{i \in C_l} ||x_i - \mu_l||^2 = \sum_{l=1}^k \sum_{i=1}^n a_{il} ||x_i - \mu_l||^2 \to minimize\]
The loss is also called within-cluster sum of squares because it sums over all squared distances of samples from the centroid to which they belong. Unknown are the cluster assignments \(a_{il}\) and the centroids \(\mu_l\).
Algorithmic challenge: given sample vectors \(x_i\), determine cluster assignment \(a_{il} \in [0,1]\) and centroids \(\mu_l\).

Expectation-Maximization-like iteration

Initialize model: \(\mu_l\)
Iterate until convergence

Assign each \(x_i\) to the cluster with nearest centroid: \(a_{il}\)
Update centroid: \(\mu_j = 1/n_j \sum_i x_i\)








The tool: KMeans()


Code
km_res = KMeans(n_clusters=3, n_init=10, random_state=RANDOM_STATE)
km_res.fit(Z)

On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.KMeans?Documentation for KMeansiFitted
        
            
                Parameters
                









n_clusters n_clusters: int, default=8

The number of clusters to form as well as the number of
centroids to generate.

For an example of how to choose an optimal value for `n_clusters` refer to
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_cluster_plot_kmeans_silhouette_analysis.py`.
3



init init: {'k-means++', 'random'}, callable or array-like of shape (n_clusters, n_features), default='k-means++'

Method for initialization:

* 'k-means++' : selects initial cluster centroids using sampling based on an empirical probability distribution of the points' contribution to the overall inertia. This technique speeds up convergence. The algorithm implemented is "greedy k-means++". It differs from the vanilla k-means++ by making several trials at each sampling step and choosing the best centroid among them.

* 'random': choose `n_clusters` observations (rows) at random from data for the initial centroids.

* If an array is passed, it should be of shape (n_clusters, n_features) and gives the initial centers.

* If a callable is passed, it should take arguments X, n_clusters and a random state and return an initialization.

For an example of how to use the different `init` strategies, see
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_cluster_plot_kmeans_digits.py`.

For an evaluation of the impact of initialization, see the example
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_cluster_plot_kmeans_stability_low_dim_dense.py`.
'k-means++'



n_init n_init: 'auto' or int, default='auto'

Number of times the k-means algorithm is run with different centroid
seeds. The final results is the best output of `n_init` consecutive runs
in terms of inertia. Several runs are recommended for sparse
high-dimensional problems (see :ref:`kmeans_sparse_high_dim`).

When `n_init='auto'`, the number of runs depends on the value of init:
10 if using `init='random'` or `init` is a callable;
1 if using `init='k-means++'` or `init` is an array-like.

.. versionadded:: 1.2
Added 'auto' option for `n_init`.

.. versionchanged:: 1.4
Default value for `n_init` changed to `'auto'`.
10



max_iter max_iter: int, default=300

Maximum number of iterations of the k-means algorithm for a
single run.
300



tol tol: float, default=1e-4

Relative tolerance with regards to Frobenius norm of the difference
in the cluster centers of two consecutive iterations to declare
convergence.
0.0001



verbose verbose: int, default=0

Verbosity mode.
0



random_state random_state: int, RandomState instance or None, default=None

Determines random number generation for centroid initialization. Use
an int to make the randomness deterministic.
See :term:`Glossary `.
42



copy_x copy_x: bool, default=True

When pre-computing distances it is more numerically accurate to center
the data first. If copy_x is True (default), then the original data is
not modified. If False, the original data is modified, and put back
before the function returns, but small numerical differences may be
introduced by subtracting and then adding the data mean. Note that if
the original data is not C-contiguous, a copy will be made even if
copy_x is False. If the original data is sparse, but not in CSR format,
a copy will be made even if copy_x is False.
True



algorithm algorithm: {"lloyd", "elkan"}, default="lloyd"

K-means algorithm to use. The classical EM-style algorithm is `"lloyd"`.
The `"elkan"` variation can be more efficient on some datasets with
well-defined clusters, by using the triangle inequality. However it's
more memory intensive due to the allocation of an extra array of shape
`(n_samples, n_clusters)`.

In scikit-learn, the total within-cluster sum of squares is called inertia_. Each cluster has its own within-cluster sum of squares, but often we are interested in the total sum over all clusters: this is what k-means minimizes for a given \(k\).
Increasing \(k\) will decrease within-cluster SS, but increase model complexity. For \(k=n\), each point would be its own centroid and within-cluster SS would be 0. Usually we look for a practical compromise.

There is no universal characterization of the best clustering. Practically, people often look for an elbow beyond which there is not much reduction in within-cluster SS.

title: Data Clustering
text:
DBSCAN: Density-Based Spatial Clustering
DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise) is a clustering algorithm that groups together points that are close to each other based on a distance metric and a minimum number of neighbors. Unlike K-Means and Hierarchical clustering, DBSCAN can find arbitrarily shaped clusters and identify noise/outliers.

Key Parameters

eps: The maximum distance between two samples for one to be considered as in the neighborhood of the other
min_samples: The number of samples in a neighborhood for a point to be considered as a core point



Advantages of DBSCAN

Can find clusters of arbitrary shape (not limited to spherical clusters like K-Means)
Robust to outliers (identifies them as noise)
Does not require specifying the number of clusters a priori
Works well with spatial data



Code
from sklearn.cluster import DBSCAN

# Use mean radius and mean texture for 2D visualization
X_2d_viz = Z[["mean radius", "mean texture"]].to_numpy()

# Try different eps values to see the effect
eps_values = [0.3, 0.5, 0.8]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=FIGSIZE)

for i, eps in enumerate(eps_values):
    dbscan = DBSCAN(eps=eps, min_samples=5)
    labels = dbscan.fit_predict(X_2d_viz)
    
    n_clusters = len(set(labels)) - (1 if -1 in labels else 0)
    n_noise = list(labels).count(-1)
    
    axes[i].scatter(X_2d_viz[:, 0], X_2d_viz[:, 1], c=labels, cmap='viridis', s=10)
    axes[i].set_title(f'eps={eps}, clusters={n_clusters}, noise={n_noise}')
    axes[i].set_xlabel('mean radius (scaled)')
    axes[i].set_ylabel('mean texture (scaled)')

plt.suptitle('DBSCAN with different eps values')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print(f"eps=0.3: {n_clusters} clusters, {n_noise} noise points")









eps=0.3: 1 clusters, 5 noise points

labels_full = dbscan_full.fit_predict(Z)

title: Data Clustering
text:
Comparing Cluster Solutions
Regardless of hierarchical clustering, k-means clustering, or other cluster algorithms, we often want to compare cluster solutions from different methods, different parameters, or pre-existing sample classifications. In general we want to compare partitions.

Visually: table and heatmap


Code
cluster_hclust = fcluster(
    linkage(Z, method="complete", metric="euclidean"),
    t=3,
    criterion="maxclust",
)
cluster_kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=3, n_init=10, random_state=RANDOM_STATE).fit_predict(Z)

ct = pd.crosstab(
    pd.Series(cluster_hclust, name="Hierarchical"),
    pd.Series(cluster_kmeans, name="KMeans"),
)

plt.figure(figsize=(7, 7))
sns.heatmap(ct, annot=True, fmt="d", cmap="Reds", cbar=False)
plt.title("Overlap of Hierarchical and K-Means cluster solutions")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()











Numerically: Adjusted Rand Index
Different cluster algorithms can produce different partitions and different numbers of clusters. Moreover, the cluster labels are arbitrary. Therefore we need a better method to compare cluster solutions. A common solution is to focus on all pairs of samples and consider how often they end up in the same cluster or different clusters, regardless of arbitrary cluster labels.
The Rand Index (Rand 1971) for two partitions is defined as follows:
\[RI = \frac{a+d}{a+b+c+d} \in [0,1]\]



Same data - two different partitions


The Adjusted Rand Index (ARI) measures the similarity between two cluster solutions, accounting for chance groupings. Specifically, it accounts for an expected RI that would be observed for random cluster assignments and for a maximally possible RI.
It ranges from -1 to 1, with 1 indicating perfect agreement and 0 indicating similarity expected by chance.
\[ARI = \frac{A - E}{M - E} \in [-1, +1]\]

\(A\) = number of cluster agreements of sample pairs
\(M\) = maximal possible number of agreements
\(E\) = expected number of agreements by chance



Code
ari_score = adjusted_rand_score(cluster_hclust, cluster_kmeans)
print(f"Adjusted Rand Index (ARI): {ari_score:.3f}")


Adjusted Rand Index (ARI): 0.327


Reference: Rand @ Wikipedia

text:
Review

Cluster goal: organize and structure data
Many suggestions

hierarchical clustering: distance + agglomeration method \(\to\) dendrogram
cut tree: dendrogram + \(k\) \(\to\) clusters
k-means: \(k\) \(\to\) centroids and clusters
DBSCAN: density-based, eps + min_samples \(\to\) arbitrary shaped clusters + noise detection
many more: see cluster analysis algorithms

Parameters: e.g. \(k\), n_init, max_iter, random seed, linkage method, eps, min_samples
Metrics:

within-cluster SS / inertia \(\to 0\)
silhouette score \(\in [-1, 1]\)
ARI \(\in [-1, 1]\)

Expert knowledge \(\to\) supervision!

Data (scaled)
The Breast Cancer Wisconsin dataset contains 30 features (obtained from images) describing characteristics of the cell nuclei, along with a binary diagnosis (malignant/benign).

Load Data


Code
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
bc_raw = load_breast_cancer(as_frame=True)


bc = bc_raw.frame.rename(columns={"target": "Diagnosis"})

# keep ann dataframe for future annotations

feature_cols = bc_raw.feature_names
X = bc[feature_cols]




Filter, Transform and Scale
Here we use the Box-Cox transform for all numerical variable (filter strictly positive means), but in practise we would look at the variables more individually and decide on a suitable transformation.
Notice; per default subsequent scaling is also part of the Box-Cox transformation implemented by PowerTransformer()


Code
from sklearn.preprocessing import PowerTransformer

# filter variables
Xs = X[[
    c for c in X
    if (c.startswith("mean")
        and (X[c] > 0).all()
        and X[c].notna().all())
]]

# Box-Cox transform (incl. scaling)
pt = PowerTransformer(method="box-cox")
Z = pt.fit_transform(Xs)
Z = pd.DataFrame(Z, columns=Xs.columns)

# Plotting for sanity check
num_norm = Z.melt(var_name="variable", value_name="value")

plt.show()

Pairwise Distances and Similarities

Between variables
\[d_{k, l} = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - Cor(Z_k, Z_l)\right) \in [0, 1]\]


Code

Between samples
\[
d_{i, j} = \left( \sum_l (Z_{il} - Z_{jl})^2 \right)^{1/2}
\]
For this to work it is important that variables are normalized
Distances between samples (=multi-variate observations) can be used to define groups of samples.

title: Data Clustering
text:
Clustering Approaches

Hierarchical Clustering
Hierarchical clustering requires:

a distance definition, for example Euclidean distance
an agglomeration method

complete linkage: cluster distance is maximal distance between cluster members
average linkage: cluster distance is average distance between cluster members

optional: cut prescription, that is, at which height to cut the tree







TipMore Agglomeration Methods





Assume that distance between all elements are defined \(d(a,b)\) and initially each elements defines its own cluster.
Clusters are merged iteratively - nearest clusters first: \(C = A\cup B\).
New distances \(D(C,X)\) between merged clusters \(C\) and old cluster \(X\) can be defined from old distances by various possible iterative updates. For example:







Method
Update rule (\(C=A\cup B\))




Single linkage
\(D(C,X)=\min\!\left(D(A,X),\,D(B,X)\right)\)


Complete linkage
\(D(C,X)=\max\!\left(D(A,X),\,D(B,X)\right)\)


WPGMA
\(D(C,X)=\displaystyle\frac{D(A,X)+D(B,X)}{2}\)


UPGMA
\(D(C,X)=\displaystyle\frac{|A|\,D(A,X)+|B|\,D(B,X)}{|A|+|B|}\)



Reference: Wikipedia Comparisons.




Tree cutting


Code
L = linkage(Z, method="complete", metric="euclidean")

# cut tree and define number of clusters
THRESH=10
cluster_hclust = fcluster(L, t=THRESH, criterion="distance")
n_clusters = len(np.unique(cluster_hclust))
ann["Clusters"] = pd.Categorical(cluster_hclust)

plt.figure(figsize=FIGSIZE)
dendrogram(L, no_labels=True, color_threshold=THRESH)
plt.title(f"Hierarchical clustering. {n_clusters} clusters @ threshold = {THRESH}")
plt.xlabel("Samples")
plt.ylabel("Distance")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





Hierarchical Clustering. Distance between samples (“leaves”) is the total length along the dendrogram (“tree”). Notice that the ordering of leaves is arbitrary.




The height at which two branches merge in the tree corresponds to the distance between the two clusters being merged, as defined by the chosen linkage criterion.
Notice that the order of branches and nodes in these hierarchical trees (“dendrograms”) is arbitrary: branches can be flipped or rotated.


Samples and Variables


Code
L_row = linkage(Z, method="complete", metric="euclidean")
L_col = linkage(Z.T, method="complete", metric = "correlation")

cluster_palette = dict(zip(
    ann["Clusters"].unique(),
    sns.color_palette("tab10", ann["Clusters"].nunique())
))

# Move colorbar next to heatmap

#plt.suptitle("Clustered data")

Hierarchical Clustering of samples and variables.










NoteInterpretation




Unsupervised clustering (with user-defined threshold!) results in 3 clusters (A, B, C)
A (blue). Mostly malignant cells with large sizes (mean radius etc) and high mean smoothness, compactness, symmetry
B (green). Mostly benign cells with small sizes and low mean smoothness
C (orange). Both benign and malignant cells with relatively large sizes and low smoothness etc.
other clustering threshold may result in different assignments or subgroups.









ImportantHierarchical Clustering is very expensive



For \(N\) samples:

Space: \(O(N^2)\) (distance matrix)
Time (naive): \(O(N^2)\) (distances) \(\times O(N)\) (merge steps)
Time (efficient): \(O(N^3) \to O(N^2)\) avoid repeated searches over all cluster pairs Sibson (1973), SLINK






k-means Clustering

A visualization
Some helper functions:


k-means implementation
def run_kmeans_history(data, k=2, max_iter=10, random_state=123):
    """
    Run a simple k-means loop and keep track of the iterations
    This is slow than the bona-fide KMeans which does not keep track of iterations

    Parameters
    ----------
    data : numpy array, shape (n_samples, n_features)
        Numerical feature matrix.
    k : int
        Number of clusters.
    max_iter : int
        Maximum number of k-means iterations.
    random_state : int
        Random seed.

    Returns
    -------
    points : DataFrame
        Original data repeated for every iteration, with cluster labels.
    centers : DataFrame
        Centroid coordinates for every iteration.
    """
    rng = np.random.default_rng(random_state)
    X = np.asarray(data)

    n_samples, n_features = X.shape

    feature_names = [f"dim_{i}" for i in range(n_features)]

    centers = rng.uniform(
        low=X.min(axis=0),
        high=X.max(axis=0),
        size=(k, n_features),
    )

    point_frames = []
    center_frames = []

    for iteration in range(1, max_iter + 1):
        # E-step: assign samples to nearest centroid
        distances = ((X[:, None, :] - centers[None, :, :]) ** 2).sum(axis=2)
        labels = distances.argmin(axis=1)

        points = pd.DataFrame(X, columns=feature_names)
        points["sample_id"] = np.arange(n_samples)
        points["cluster"] = labels.astype(str)
        points["iteration"] = iteration
        point_frames.append(points)

        centroids = pd.DataFrame(centers, columns=feature_names)
        centroids["sample_id"] = "centroid_" + pd.Series(range(k)).astype(str)
        centroids["cluster"] = [str(i) for i in range(k)]
        centroids["iteration"] = iteration
        center_frames.append(centroids)

        # M-step: update centroid positions
        new_centers = centers.copy()

        for j in range(k):
            if np.any(labels == j):
                new_centers[j] = X[labels == j].mean(axis=0)

        if np.allclose(new_centers, centers):
            break

        centers = new_centers

    return (
        pd.concat(point_frames, ignore_index=True),
        pd.concat(center_frames, ignore_index=True),
    )




Run k-means
# Select 2 meaningful features for visualization: mean radius and mean texture
X_2d = Z[["mean radius", "mean texture"]].to_numpy()

points, centers = run_kmeans_history(
    X_2d,
    k=3,
    max_iter=10,
    random_state=42,
)




Animated Plot
points["kind"] = "data point"
centers["kind"] = "centroid"
plot_df = pd.concat([points, centers], ignore_index=True)

# Rename dimensions for clarity
plot_df = plot_df.rename(columns={"dim_0": "mean radius (scaled)", "dim_1": "mean texture (scaled)"})

fig = px.scatter(
    plot_df,
    x="mean radius (scaled)",
    y="mean texture (scaled)",
    color="cluster",

        "data point": "circle",

\(D=2\) is chosen only for illustration. In general distances and centroids are defined in any dimensions \(D\)








ImportantComputational Cost



\(N\) observations, \(D\) dimensions, \(K\) clusters

Space: \(N\) observations + \(K\) cluster centroids \(\to O(N+K)\)
Time: \(O(NKD)\) / iterations (find nearest centroid) \(\to O(N)\)
\(K\) (number of clsuters) and \(I\) (number of iterations) are usually small









NoteLoss Function and Some Maths





The Loss Function
\[L = \sum_{l=1}^k \sum_{i \in C_l} ||x_i - \mu_l||^2 = \sum_{l=1}^k \sum_{i=1}^n a_{il} ||x_i - \mu_l||^2 \to minimize\]
The loss is also called within-cluster sum of squares because it sums over all squared distances of samples from the centroid to which they belong. Unknown are the cluster assignments \(a_{il}\) and the centroids \(\mu_l\).
Algorithmic challenge: given sample vectors \(x_i\), determine cluster assignment \(a_{il} \in [0,1]\) and centroids \(\mu_l\).

Expectation-Maximization-like iteration

Initialize model: \(\mu_l\)
Iterate until convergence

Assign each \(x_i\) to the cluster with nearest centroid: \(a_{il}\)
Update centroid: \(\mu_j = 1/n_j \sum_i x_i\)








The tool: KMeans()
With Python/scikit-learn the implementation is very easy


Code
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
km_res = KMeans(n_clusters=3, n_init=10, random_state=RANDOM_STATE)
km_res.fit(Z)


KMeans(n_clusters=3, n_init=10, random_state=42)In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook. On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.KMeans?Documentation for KMeansiFitted
        
            
                Parameters
                









n_clusters n_clusters: int, default=8

The number of clusters to form as well as the number of
centroids to generate.

For an example of how to choose an optimal value for `n_clusters` refer to
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_cluster_plot_kmeans_silhouette_analysis.py`.
3



init init: {'k-means++', 'random'}, callable or array-like of shape (n_clusters, n_features), default='k-means++'

Method for initialization:

* 'k-means++' : selects initial cluster centroids using sampling based on an empirical probability distribution of the points' contribution to the overall inertia. This technique speeds up convergence. The algorithm implemented is "greedy k-means++". It differs from the vanilla k-means++ by making several trials at each sampling step and choosing the best centroid among them.

* 'random': choose `n_clusters` observations (rows) at random from data for the initial centroids.

* If an array is passed, it should be of shape (n_clusters, n_features) and gives the initial centers.

* If a callable is passed, it should take arguments X, n_clusters and a random state and return an initialization.

For an example of how to use the different `init` strategies, see
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_cluster_plot_kmeans_digits.py`.

For an evaluation of the impact of initialization, see the example
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_cluster_plot_kmeans_stability_low_dim_dense.py`.
'k-means++'



n_init n_init: 'auto' or int, default='auto'

Number of times the k-means algorithm is run with different centroid
seeds. The final results is the best output of `n_init` consecutive runs
in terms of inertia. Several runs are recommended for sparse
high-dimensional problems (see :ref:`kmeans_sparse_high_dim`).

When `n_init='auto'`, the number of runs depends on the value of init:
10 if using `init='random'` or `init` is a callable;
1 if using `init='k-means++'` or `init` is an array-like.

.. versionchanged:: 1.4

max_iter max_iter: int, default=300

Maximum number of iterations of the k-means algorithm for a
single run.
300



tol tol: float, default=1e-4

Relative tolerance with regards to Frobenius norm of the difference
in the cluster centers of two consecutive iterations to declare
convergence.
0.0001



verbose verbose: int, default=0

Verbosity mode.
0



random_state random_state: int, RandomState instance or None, default=None

Determines random number generation for centroid initialization. Use
an int to make the randomness deterministic.
See :term:`Glossary `.
42



copy_x copy_x: bool, default=True

When pre-computing distances it is more numerically accurate to center
the data first. If copy_x is True (default), then the original data is
not modified. If False, the original data is modified, and put back
before the function returns, but small numerical differences may be
introduced by subtracting and then adding the data mean. Note that if
the original data is not C-contiguous, a copy will be made even if
copy_x is False. If the original data is sparse, but not in CSR format,
a copy will be made even if copy_x is False.
True



algorithm algorithm: {"lloyd", "elkan"}, default="lloyd"

K-means algorithm to use. The classical EM-style algorithm is `"lloyd"`.
The `"elkan"` variation can be more efficient on some datasets with
well-defined clusters, by using the triangle inequality. However it's
more memory intensive due to the allocation of an extra array of shape
`(n_samples, n_clusters)`.

5 rows × 32 columns



In scikit-learn, the total within-cluster sum of squares is called inertia_. Each cluster has its own within-cluster sum of squares, but often we are interested in the total sum over all clusters: this is what k-means minimizes for a given \(k\).


Screening cluster solutions
Increasing \(k\) will decrease within-cluster SS, but increase model complexity. For \(k=n\), each point would be its own centroid and within-cluster SS would be 0. Usually we look for a practical compromise.


Code
k_values = range(1, 21)
res_kmeans = [
    KMeans(n_clusters=k, n_init=10, random_state=RANDOM_STATE).fit(Z)
    for k in k_values
]

screening = pd.DataFrame({
    "k": list(k_values),
    "within_ss": [model.inertia_ for model in res_kmeans],
})

plt.figure(figsize=FIGSIZE)
sns.lineplot(data=screening, x="k", y="within_ss", marker="o")
plt.title("Within-cluster SS vs k")
plt.xlabel("Number of clusters k")
plt.ylabel("Total within-cluster SS (inertia)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





screen clustering for a range of values k




There is no universal characterization of the best clustering. Practically, people often look for an elbow beyond which there is not much reduction in within-cluster SS.
For a given cluster solution, each cluster has their own within-cluster SS


Code

withinss

Cluster Evaluation: Silhouette analysis
The silhouette score measures how well each data point fits within its assigned cluster compared to other clusters. It quantifies cohesion and separation. The value for each data point ranges from -1 to 1.
A formal definition is given here: wiki


Code

title: Data Clustering
text:
Comparing Cluster Solutions
Regardless of hierarchical clustering, k-means clustering, or other cluster algorithms, we often want to compare cluster solutions from different methods, different parameters, or pre-existing sample classifications. In general we want to compare partitions.

Visually: table and heatmap


Code
cluster_hclust = fcluster(
    linkage(Z, method="complete", metric="euclidean"),
    t=3,
    criterion="maxclust",
)
cluster_kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=3, n_init=10, random_state=RANDOM_STATE).fit_predict(Z)

ct = pd.crosstab(
    pd.Series(cluster_hclust, name="Hierarchical"),
    pd.Series(cluster_kmeans, name="KMeans"),
)

plt.figure(figsize=(7, 7))
sns.heatmap(ct, annot=True, fmt="d", cmap="Reds", cbar=False)
plt.title("Overlap of Hierarchical and K-Means cluster solutions")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()











Numerically: Adjusted Rand Index
Different cluster algorithms can produce different partitions and different numbers of clusters. Moreover, the cluster labels are arbitrary. Therefore we need a better method to compare cluster solutions. A common solution is to focus on all pairs of samples and consider how often they end up in the same cluster or different clusters, regardless of arbitrary cluster labels.
The Rand Index (Rand 1971) for two partitions is defined as follows:
\[RI = \frac{a+d}{a+b+c+d} \in [0,1]\]



Same data - two different partitions


The Adjusted Rand Index (ARI) measures the similarity between two cluster solutions, accounting for chance groupings. Specifically, it accounts for an expected RI that would be observed for random cluster assignments and for a maximally possible RI.
It ranges from -1 to 1, with 1 indicating perfect agreement and 0 indicating similarity expected by chance.
\[ARI = \frac{A - E}{M - E} \in [-1, +1]\]

\(A\) = number of cluster agreements of sample pairs
\(M\) = maximal possible number of agreements
\(E\) = expected number of agreements by chance



Code
ari_score = adjusted_rand_score(cluster_hclust, cluster_kmeans)
print(f"Adjusted Rand Index (ARI): {ari_score:.3f}")


Adjusted Rand Index (ARI): 0.327


Reference: Rand @ Wikipedia

Data (scaled)
The Breast Cancer Wisconsin dataset contains 30 features (obtained from images) describing characteristics of the cell nuclei, along with a binary diagnosis (malignant/benign).

Load Data


Code
bc_raw = load_breast_cancer(as_frame=True)
bc = bc_raw.frame.rename(columns={"target": "Diagnosis"})
bc["Diagnosis"] = bc["Diagnosis"].map({0: "malignant", 1: "benign"})

# keep ann dataframe for future annotations
ann = bc[["Diagnosis"]].copy()

feature_cols = bc_raw.feature_names
X = bc[feature_cols]




Transform and Scale Data
Here we use the Box-Cox transform for all numerical variable (filter strictly positive means), but in practise we would probably look at the variables more individually and decide on a suitable transformation.
Notice that per default subsequent scaling is also part of the Box-Cox transformation implemented by PowerTransformer()


Code
from sklearn.preprocessing import PowerTransformer

# filter variables
Xs = X[[
    c for c in X
    if (c.startswith("mean")
        and (X[c] > 0).all()
        and X[c].notna().all())
]]

# Box-Cox transform
pt = PowerTransformer(method="box-cox")
Z = pt.fit_transform(Xs)
Z = pd.DataFrame(Z, columns=Xs.columns)

# Plotting for sanity check
num_norm = Z.melt(var_name="variable", value_name="value")

plt.figure(figsize=FIGSIZE)
sns.violinplot(data=num_norm, x="variable", y="value")
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.ylabel("Z")
plt.title("Transformed and Standardized Variables")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: DBSCAN
text:
DBSCAN: Density-Based Spatial Clustering
DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise) is a clustering algorithm that groups together points that are close to each other based on a distance metric and a minimum number of neighbors. Unlike K-Means and Hierarchical clustering, DBSCAN can find arbitrarily shaped clusters and identify noise/outliers.

Key Parameters

eps: The maximum distance between two samples for one to be considered as in the neighborhood of the other
min_samples: The number of samples in a neighborhood for a point to be considered as a core point



Advantages of DBSCAN

Can find clusters of arbitrary shape (not limited to spherical clusters like K-Means)
Robust to outliers (identifies them as noise)
Does not require specifying the number of clusters a priori
Works well with spatial data



Code
from sklearn.cluster import DBSCAN

# Use mean radius and mean texture for 2D visualization
X_2d_viz = Z[["mean radius", "mean texture"]].to_numpy()

# Try different eps values to see the effect
eps_values = [0.3, 0.5, 0.8]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=FIGSIZE)

for i, eps in enumerate(eps_values):
    dbscan = DBSCAN(eps=eps, min_samples=5)
    labels = dbscan.fit_predict(X_2d_viz)
    
    n_clusters = len(set(labels)) - (1 if -1 in labels else 0)
    n_noise = list(labels).count(-1)
    
    axes[i].scatter(X_2d_viz[:, 0], X_2d_viz[:, 1], c=labels, cmap='viridis', s=10)
    axes[i].set_title(f'eps={eps}, clusters={n_clusters}, noise={n_noise}')
    axes[i].set_xlabel('mean radius (scaled)')
    axes[i].set_ylabel('mean texture (scaled)')

plt.suptitle('DBSCAN with different eps values')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print(f"eps=0.3: {n_clusters} clusters, {n_noise} noise points")









eps=0.3: 1 clusters, 5 noise points




Code
# Run DBSCAN on all 30 features with tuned parameters
dbscan_full = DBSCAN(eps=1, min_samples=10)
labels_full = dbscan_full.fit_predict(Z)

n_clusters_full = len(set(labels_full)) - (1 if -1 in labels_full else 0)
n_noise_full = list(labels_full).count(-1)

print(f"DBSCAN on all 30 features: {n_clusters_full} clusters, {n_noise_full} noise points")

# Compare with diagnosis
if n_clusters_full >= 2:
    ari_dbscan = adjusted_rand_score(bc["Diagnosis"].map({"malignant": 0, "benign": 1}), labels_full)
    print(f"Adjusted Rand Index vs Diagnosis: {ari_dbscan:.3f}")


DBSCAN on all 30 features: 1 clusters, 349 noise points

Encoding Variable Levels

Levels

ImportantQuestion



How should we transform categorical variables?




Boolean Encoding


Code
# Define a subset X for illustration
X = pd.DataFrame({
    "Gender": df["Gender"] == "Male",
    "HighCalories": df["HighCalories"] == "yes",
    "Smoking": df["Smoking"] == "yes"
})

print(f'X: \n {X.head()}')


X:

1   False         False     True

4    True         False    False








NoteBoolean Convenience



Boolean is very suitable to encode binary variables, and define subsets (e.g for UpSet plots)
The implicit equivalence (False=0, True=1) also helps to perform basic calculations such as cooccurence and correlation analysis
\[
X \to Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}
\]
\[
C = X^T \cdot X ~~~~~~~~~~~ R = Z^T \cdot Z
\]




Code
C = X.astype(int).T @ X.astype(int)
print(f'Co-occurence matrix: \n{C}')

Z = (X - X.mean()) / X.std()
print(f'X -> Z: \n {Z.head()}')  

R = Z.T @ Z / (len(Z) - 1)  # R = X.corr()
print(f'Correlation Matrix: \n{R}')

HighCalories  0.064934      1.000000 -0.050660

Integer Encoding
It might be tempting (and easy) to apply integer encoding to categorical variables with more levels


Code
X = pd.DataFrame({
    "Gender": df["Gender"].astype("category").cat.codes,
    "Obesity": df["Obesity"].astype("category").cat.codes,
    "Weight": df["Weight"]
})

print(f'X: \n {X.head()}')

    data=X,
    x="Obesity",
    y="Weight",
    alpha=0.3
)

plt.title('Be careful with integer encoding')
plt.xlabel("Obesity (integer encoded)")
plt.show()


X: 
    Gender  Obesity  Weight
0       0        1    64.0
1       0        1    56.0
2       1        1    77.0
3       1        5    87.0
4       1        6    89.8















WarningWarning




Integer encoding can be misleading for categorical variables
sorting, distances and means are not usually meaningful
need higher dimensional space





One-hot Encoding
The key idea is to replace each category level by a different vector, in a \(K\)-dimensional space (for \(K\) categories). For convenience these vectors are normalized and orthogonal.


Code
VAR="Obesity" 
print(f'Number of {VAR} levels: {df[VAR].nunique()}')
VAR_onehot = pd.get_dummies(df[VAR], drop_first=False)  

sns.heatmap(VAR_onehot[:15], cmap="Greys", cbar=False, annot=False)
plt.title(f'One-hot encoded {VAR}. Shape: {VAR_onehot.shape}. Rows sums=1')
plt.ylabel('Observation')
plt.show() 


Number of Obesity levels: 7











Code
VAR_onehot = VAR_onehot.astype(int)  # ensure integer type for dot product
sns.heatmap(
    VAR_onehot.T @ VAR_onehot,
    annot=True,
    cmap="Blues",
    fmt="d"
)

plt.title(f"Orthogonality of one-hot encoded {VAR}")
plt.show()









Each row \(i\) sums to 1 (over \(k\) levels): \[
\sum_{j=1}^{k} x_{ij} = 1
\]
Columns \(j\) and \(l\) are orthogonal to each other \[
\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij} x_{il} = n_j \delta_{jl}
\]






ImportantTask




replace “Obesity” with “Transport” and interpret
apply one-hot encoding with drop_first = True









TipOne-hot encoding with sklearn





sklearn also provides a convenient OneHotEncoder which can be used in a pipeline over multiple categorical variables


Code
from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder

encoder = OneHotEncoder(sparse_output=False, drop="first") 
X_encoded = encoder.fit_transform(df[cat_cols]) 

# back to pandas dataframe for visualization
X_encoded_df = pd.DataFrame(
    X_encoded,
    columns=encoder.get_feature_names_out(cat_cols),
    index=df.index
)

sns.heatmap(X_encoded_df.iloc[:10], cmap="Greys", cbar=False, annot=True)
plt.title(f'One-hot encoded. Shape: {X_encoded_df.shape}.')

title: Encoding Categorical Data
text:
Relation between categorical variables

Crosstabulation
The one-hot encoding representation is also convenient to formalize cross-tabulation as matrix multiplication: \[
C = A^T B =  \text{crosstab}(A, B) \longrightarrow
\]
\[
C_{kj} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_{ik} B_{ij} =
\]


Code
VAR1 = "Gender"
VAR2 = "Obesity"
#A = pd.get_dummies(df[VAR1]).astype(int)
#B = pd.get_dummies(df[VAR2]).astype(int)
#CT = A.T @ B

# contingency table = crosstab
CT = pd.crosstab(df[VAR1], df[VAR2])  

sns.heatmap(
    CT,
    annot=True,
    cmap="Blues",
    fmt="d"
)
plt.title(f"Crosstab of {VAR1} and {VAR2}")
plt.show()















TipMosaic Plot







Code
from statsmodels.graphics.mosaicplot import mosaic
mosaic(df, [VAR1, VAR2], gap=0.01)
plt.title(f"{VAR1} × {VAR2}")
plt.show()














Metrics






NoteQuestion



How should we quantify and assess a given observed overlap \(O_{ij}\)?


Given a contingency table of observed counts \(O_{ij}\) and the total count over all cells \(N\), we can calculate the expected counts for a given overlap from the marginal distributions
Expected Counts: \[
E_{ij}=N*Pr(row=i)*Pr(col=j)
\]
\(\chi^2\) Metrics:
\[
\chi^2 = \sum_i \sum_j \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}
\]
This can be translated into \(P\)-values


Code
from scipy.stats import chi2_contingency

# chi-squared test of independence
chi2, p_value, dof, expected = chi2_contingency(CT)

sns.heatmap(expected, annot=True, fmt=".1f",cmap="Blues")
plt.title(f"Expected Counts")
plt.xlabel(VAR2)
plt.ylabel(VAR1)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

pearson_residuals = (CT - expected) / np.sqrt(expected)
sns.heatmap(
    pearson_residuals,
    annot=True,
    fmt=".2f",
    cmap="coolwarm",
    center=0
)

plt.title(f"Pearson residuals. P={p_value:.2e}")
plt.xlabel(VAR2)
plt.ylabel(VAR1)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
